steps.title: Identify the rhetorical goal
steps.content: The student wants to introduce both Marcus Baptiste and his novel The Lonely Londoners to a new audience. The effective choice must name Baptiste, identify him, name the novel, and give the audience enough context to know what it is about.

steps.title: Test the candidates against the goal
steps.content: The right choice will pack in author identity (Trinidadian), novel title, publication year, and subject (post-WWII Caribbean migration to Britain) in a single introductory sentence.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — In 1956, Trinidadian author Marcus Baptiste published one of his most celebrated novels, The Lonely Londoners, which is about a group of men who emigrate from the Caribbean to Great Britain after World War II.: names Baptiste, identifies him as Trinidadian, names the novel, gives the year, and summarizes its subject. ✓
  • Choice B — Marcus Baptiste wrote the novel Moses Ascending after he wrote The Lonely Londoners.: misreads the goal — it focuses on a later novel and tells a new audience nothing about who Baptiste is or what The Lonely Londoners is about. ✗
  • Choice C — The Lonely Londoners, a celebrated novel that was published in 1956, depicts post–World War II Caribbean migration from the perspective of a Trinidadian author.: never names Marcus Baptiste, so it fails to introduce the author. ✗
  • Choice D — Some of the characters who appear in Marcus Baptiste's Moses Ascending also appear in The Lonely Londoners.: misreads the goal — it is an intertextual detail, not an introduction, and gives no information about Baptiste or what the novel is about. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
